Per-Trial Results: Gregory · Rebecca · ChatGPT · Copilot   Exp 1 — balanced_names — LLaMA-2-13B-Chat  |  Generated: 2026-02-21 18:11:12

Statistical Methods

  • N = 50 LLM subjects, each completing 10 conversations per agent (40 total: 4 agents × 10 topics).
  • Unit of analysis: per-subject, per-agent mean (aggregated over 10 trials). All tests are within-subject (paired) because every subject conversed with all four agents.
  • Omnibus test: One-way repeated-measures ANOVA (k=4 agents; df = (3, 147) for N=50). Effect size: η² (eta-squared, SS_between / SS_total).
  • Post-hoc pairwise tests: Six paired t-tests per metric, Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) FDR correction applied within each metric's six comparisons. Effect size: Cohen's dz (mean of differences / SD of differences).
  • Aggregate contrasts (3 per metric, BH-corrected within this family):
    (a) Human mean (Gregory+Rebecca) vs AI mean (ChatGPT+Copilot) — type replication;
    (b) Gregory vs AI mean;   (c) Rebecca vs AI mean.
  • Uneven trial note: When an individual agent (10 trials/subject) is compared to the AI aggregate (mean of ChatGPT+Copilot = 20 trials/subject), the AI estimate is more stable, but the test is a valid paired comparison at N=50. This is flagged wherever it applies.
  • Significance: * padj < .05, ** < .01, *** < .001.

1 · Human vs AI Summary (averaged across individual partners)

Human mean = average of Gregory, Rebecca; AI mean = average of ChatGPT, Copilot. Paired t-tests with BH-FDR correction across all 23 metrics. 10 significant after correction.

Positive values (red) = row metric is higher for the first agent in the pair; negative (blue) = higher for the second. Asterisks indicate BH-FDR significance.

Brighter colour = more significant. Asterisks as above.

6 · Interpretation

Overall RM-ANOVA: agent-level differences

12 of 23 metrics showed a significant omnibus effect (p < .05): Questions (#), Demir: Epistemic Adverbs, Demir: Epistemic Adj., Fung: Interpersonal DMs, Fung: Cognitive DMs, Fung: Discourse Markers (Total), Filler Rate (LIWC), Disfluency Rate (Total), Discourse 'Like' Rate, ToM Phrases Rate, Politeness Rate, Connectedness (1–4).

Within-human variation (Gregory vs Rebecca)

Significant after BH-FDR in 3 metric(s):

  • Filler Rate (LIWC)
  • Disfluency Rate (Total)
  • Connectedness (1–4)

Within-AI variation (ChatGPT vs Copilot)

Significant after BH-FDR in 9 metric(s):

  • Questions (#)
  • Fung: Interpersonal DMs
  • Fung: Cognitive DMs
  • Fung: Discourse Markers (Total)
  • Filler Rate (LIWC)
  • Disfluency Rate (Total)
  • Discourse 'Like' Rate
  • ToM Phrases Rate
  • Connectedness (1–4)

Cross-type variation (any human vs any AI, post-hoc)

At least one human–AI pairwise comparison significant in 11 metric(s):

  • Questions (#)
  • Demir: Epistemic Adj.
  • Fung: Interpersonal DMs
  • Fung: Cognitive DMs
  • Fung: Discourse Markers (Total)
  • Filler Rate (LIWC)
  • Disfluency Rate (Total)
  • Discourse 'Like' Rate
  • ToM Phrases Rate
  • Politeness Rate
  • Connectedness (1–4)

Interpretation notes

  • Significant within-human or within-AI differences would suggest the model responds to specific identities, not merely the type label. Gender (Gregory vs Rebecca) and brand (ChatGPT vs Copilot) are the relevant contrasts.
  • If cross-type effects are larger than within-type effects, the primary driver is the human/AI distinction, not individual identity.
  • The dz heatmap provides the clearest summary of which pairs drive any omnibus effect and the magnitude of those differences.
